title: Jerusalem artichoke soup
subtitle: Nutty, creamy, and distinctively earthy
excerpt: Jerusalem artichokes blended with shallots, thyme, and chicken stock into a rich, nutty soup with a distinctive earthy flavour. One of the most interesting winter soups there is, and one of the simplest to make.
sourceNotes: Jerusalem artichoke soup is a British winter standard. The Jerusalem artichoke (Helianthus tuberosus) is native to North America and arrived in Britain in the 17th century. No single canonical source; method synthesised from standard British soup approaches.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before adding cream. Keeps for up to two months. The flavour holds well.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to two days ahead. Reheat gently and add cream when hot.

glossaryTerms.definition: A knobbly tuber from a plant related to the sunflower, with no connection to Jerusalem or to globe artichokes. The name comes from a corruption of the Italian 'girasole' (sunflower). Its earthy, nutty flavour is unlike any other vegetable.

content.text: Jerusalem artichoke soup has one of the most distinctive flavours of any British winter vegetable soup: earthy, nutty, and slightly sweet. The

is knobbly and slightly awkward to peel, but the effort is worth it. The tubers blend beautifully into a thick, velvety soup that needs very little else: a little shallot, thyme, good stock, and cream. Serve with crispy croutons or a fried sage leaf on top.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{shallot}} shallots and cook for 6 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and {{thyme}} of thyme and cook for 1 minute.

Add the {{jerusalem-artichoke}} of peeled, chopped Jerusalem artichokes. Stir to coat in the butter. Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of hot stock. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook for 20 minutes until the artichokes are completely tender.
Remove the thyme stalks. Blend until smooth. Stir in {{double-cream}} of cream and the juice of {{lemon}} lemon. Taste and season with salt.
Ladle into bowls. Add a swirl of cream and a grind of black pepper.

items.symptom: Peeling the artichokes is very difficult
items.cause: Jerusalem artichokes are knobbly and hard to peel with a standard peeler.
items.fix: Use a small paring knife rather than a peeler. Alternatively, scrub well, roast whole at 200°C for 40 minutes, then scoop out the flesh ,  the skin acts as its own container.

items.symptom: Soup is very starchy and thick
items.cause: Jerusalem artichokes are high in inulin, a starchy fibre, which gives the soup body but can make it gluey if blended too long.
items.fix: Thin with a little hot stock or water. Stop blending as soon as the texture is smooth.

items.cause: Jerusalem artichokes have a pronounced earthy flavour that some find strong.
items.fix: The lemon juice is essential and must not be skipped. It brightens the earthiness considerably. A little more cream also helps round the flavour.

items.cause: Jerusalem artichokes oxidise quickly once cut and can turn grey if not used promptly.
items.fix: Drop peeled and chopped pieces into cold water with a squeeze of lemon juice as you go. Drain and use straightaway.

content.text: Jerusalem artichokes arrived in Britain from North America in the early 17th century. They were widely grown, then fell out of fashion in the 18th century, and returned to restaurant menus in the 1980s. The soup is one of the best uses of the vegetable. The flavour comes through clearly and the result consistently surprises people.
